articles.author: Lorian Belanger, Brian P. D. Hannon
articles.description: U.S. President Donald Trump said the war in Iran was “very close to over” in clips of an interview set to air Wednesday, while the U.S. military said its blockade on Iranian ports had been “fully implemented.”
articles.title: Live updates: US blockade of Iranian ports 'fully implemented' as Trump says war near end

articles.author: Munir Ahmed, Sam Metz
articles.description: Hopes rose for renewed talks between the United States and Iran as the U.S. military said its blockade of Iranian ports was in full effect and Tehran threatened to retaliate by strike targets across the war-weary region. U.S. President Donald Trump said Tuesday that a second round of talks could happen “over the next two days" as diplomats worked through back channels to arrange them. U.N. Secretary-General António Guterres concurred, saying it’s “highly probable” that talks will restart, citing a meeting he had with Pakistan’s deputy prime minister, Ishaq Dar. The war, now in its seventh week, has jolted markets and rattled the global economy as shipping has been cut off and airstrikes have torn through military and civilian infrastructure across the region.
articles.title: Hopes rise for more negotiations as US military says Iran blockade in effect

articles.author: David Klepper
articles.description: Congress is set to take up the reauthorization of a divisive program that lets U.S. spy agencies pore over foreigners’ calls, texts and emails. A key provision of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act allows the agencies to collect and analyze overseas communications without a warrant. The conversations of Americans interacting with foreign targets can be swept up. The program expires Monday, and critics want changes such as requiring warrants to access Americans' communications. But significant updates seem unlikely after President Donald Trump announced his support Tuesday for renewing the program.
articles.title: Trump urges extending foreign surveillance program

articles.author: Kareem Chehayeb, Abby Sewell
articles.description: Lebanon and Israel have started direct talks for the first time in decades, driven by the recent war involving Hezbollah. Officials are cautious about the outcomes, but the talks are a significant step. The two countries have had a rocky relationship since 1948, with no formal diplomatic ties. The current Lebanese government, which came to power in 2025, is pushing for disarmament of non-state actors like Hezbollah. The talks began Tuesday in Washington, focusing on logistics. Lebanon seeks a ceasefire and Israeli withdrawal, while Israel wants to disarm Hezbollah. Past negotiations have often failed to produce lasting agreements.
articles.title: What to know about the Lebanon-Israel talks

articles.author: Joey Cappelletti, Julia Demaree-nikhinson
articles.description: Dearborn, Michigan, home to the nation's largest Arab American community, is grappling with ongoing crises. Families are anxious about relatives in Lebanon, which has been engulfed by the widening conflict with Iran. This anxiety is compounded by domestic pressures such as immigration enforcement and economic challenges. Many community leaders who supported President Donald Trump in the 2024 election say they would not back Republicans again. The conflict abroad, especially between Israel and Hezbollah, has intensified, displacing over a million people in Lebanon. For Dearborn, it is a city in mourning.
articles.title: Trump vowed peace in the Middle East. In Dearborn, it feels farther away

articles.author: Colleen Barry
articles.description: Italian Premier Giorgia Meloni's relationship with Donald Trump appears strained. Trump criticized Meloni for not supporting his stance on Iran and for calling his attack on Pope Leo XIV “unacceptable.” Meloni hasn't directly responded, but experts suggest this may benefit her politically. Trump expressed disappointment, saying their relationship has changed. Meloni's recent actions, including refusing U.S. bombers landing rights in Sicily, highlight her distancing from Trump. Despite this, Italy's alliance with the U.S. remains intact. Meloni is focusing on domestic issues, especially energy, amid challenges following a referendum defeat.
articles.title: Italian Premier Meloni could benefit at home from rift with Trump

articles.author: The Associated Press
articles.description: President Donald Trump has again threatened to fire Jerome Powell if the Federal Reserve Chair decides to stay on the central bank’s governing board after his term as chair expires next month. “Well then I’ll have to fire him, OK?” Trump told Fox Business in an interview that aired Wednesday when reminded that Powell has said he won’t leave with an investigation still pending against him. Powell said he would remain as chair of the Fed’s interest rate-setting committee after his term ends if no successor has been confirmed. And he said he could stay even longer if the investigation continues. Trump has for months wanted to remove Powell as chair of the Fed.
articles.title: Trump threatens to fire Powell if the Fed Chair remains with central bank after his term ends

articles.author: Fatima Hussein
articles.description: The tax filing deadline for most Americans is Wednesday, and the Trump administration is highlighting the impacts of Republicans' massive tax and spending law. The law includes eliminating taxes on tips and overtime, exemptions for interest on certain car loans, and new deductions for some seniors. The administration says more than 53 million filers claimed deductions under these provisions, as of Tuesday. Despite these changes, recent polling shows that 7 in 10 Americans still think their taxes are too high. The IRS has faced leadership turnover and workforce reductions, and on Wednesday, its CEO Frank Bisignano is set to testify before the Senate Finance Committee.
articles.title: It's Tax Day, the deadline for filing 2025 taxes

articles.author: Stan Choe
articles.description: U.S. stocks are flirting with a record following their big rally over the last two weeks. The S&P 500 rose 0.1% Wednesday and at one point was on track to eclipse its record set in January. The Dow Jones Industrial Average dipped 174 points, and the Nasdaq composite gained 0.5%. The U.S. bond market also held steady as mediators moved closer to extending the U.S.-Iran ceasefire and restarting negotiations. After falling nearly 10% below its record in late March, the S&P 500 has roared 10% higher on hopes the global economy can avoid a worst-case scenario because of the U.S.-Iran war.
articles.title: Wall Street flirts with a record after its big rally the last 2 weeks

articles.author: Eduardo Verdugo, María Verza
articles.description: The Guerreros Buscadores and dozens of other groups in Mexico scour the country for people who are missing. More than 130,000 people have been reported missing since 2006, according to official records. It is perilous work in a perilous environment. Mexico is neither at war nor under a military dictatorship, yet thousands of people disappear every year amid cartel violence. Clandestine graves are discovered on a semiregular basis; more than 70,000 unidentified remains have piled up in morgues and cemeteries.
articles.title: Citizen groups scour Mexican cartel lands for the missing
